hero.title: Matang Town
hero.subtitle: Dianbai, Maoming, Guangdong · An ancient town between mountain and sea

home.overview1: Matang Town is part of Dianbai District, Maoming City, Guangdong. It lies in the southeast of Dianbai on the western Guangdong coast, with hills and mountains behind and the sea in front. Legend says the name comes from an immortal's horse leaving hoof prints on a bluestone in the street. The town has a long history and a strong sense of community.
home.overview2: The registered population is about 94,000. Market days (lunar 3rd, 6th, 9th) draw large crowds. Agriculture, granite quarrying, and lock-making are mainstays. Changshan Village, Gongluoling, and Chaowu Cave carry revolutionary and cultural memory. The town is a window on Dianbai's local culture and rural revitalisation.
home.overview3: Matang is not a place known for a single attraction. Its value lies in the combination of eastern Dianbai gateway, agricultural heartland, hilly pastoral landscape, and traditional village culture. From Nianli folk festivals to revolutionary sites, from Simiao rice to selenium-rich produce, Matang's stories are scattered across villages, ancestral halls, temples, and farming life.
home.card1Desc: Location, natural environment, history, transport hub and local specialties
home.card2Desc: Population, local culture and food traditions
home.card3Desc: Nianli festival, folk beliefs and Liu Xian San Tai Temple

location.title: Location
location.p1: Matang Town lies in the southeast of Dianbai District, Maoming City, Guangdong, on the western Guangdong coast. It borders Rudong Town of Yangxi County to the east across the river, Lingmen Town to the southeast, Diancheng Town to the south, Guanzhu Town to the west, and Wangfu Town to the north. The total area is about 161.91 sq km.
location.p2: The area is rich in minerals including gold, tin, and tungsten, with granite reserves of about 1 billion cubic metres, making it an important resource and agricultural town in Dianbai.

nature.title: Natural Environment
nature.p1: The terrain is mainly hills and mountains, higher in the west and lower in the east. Notable peaks include Jiangjun Ridge, Nanlei Ridge, Yitan Ridge, and Jian Ridge. Rivers flow roughly fan-shaped eastward into the Rudong River, creating a landscape of forested hills alongside flat farmland.
nature.p2: Matang has a strong ecological foundation with high forest coverage. Its pastoral village scenery is typical of the region, making it one of the most distinctly rural and scenic areas in Dianbai.

history-layout-villages.title: History, Layout & Villages
history-layout-villages.p1: Matang's administrative history: it was part of District One in 1953, became Matang People's Commune in 1958, Matang District in 1983, and Matang Town in 1987. The name "Matang" (horse hoof) is widely said to come from a legend of an immortal's horse leaving hoof prints on a stone—an image even incorporated into the design of Matang Station.
history-layout-villages.p2: The town's spatial pattern can be summarised as "market core, ringed villages, and roads as links." The market area serves commerce and public services, while surrounding villages are distributed around farmland, waterways, and road corridors, creating a mutually supportive rural living-and-production structure.
history-layout-villages.p3: Matang governs 1 community and 19 village committees, including Matang Community, Matang Village, Changshan Village, Xiashan Village, Luyue Village, Shangcun Village, Shihu Village, Fengmen Village, Tianxing Village, Shigubay Village, Shanhu Village, Lianqun Village, Huangjiang Village, Huatian Village, Xiutian Village, Longwan Village, Xiaohe Village, Lianhe Village, Xiadongling Village, and Songtang Village.

transport.title: Transport
transport.p1: Matang Station is in Matang Town on the Shenzhen–Maoming railway and is an important stop on the Guangzhou–Zhanjiang high-speed line, with 2 platforms and 4 tracks. It is about 292 km from Guangzhou South and about 140 km from Zhanjiang West, a key node on the western Guangdong coastal rail corridor.
transport.p2: The Shuixia Road (Matang section) opened in August 2024, linking Bole Port first-class road with Provincial Road S386 north to Matang Station, providing fast access between Bole Port and the high-speed rail station for port–rail interchange. S386 and other trunk roads connect the town with Shuidong, Diancheng, Shalang, and nearby towns.
transport.p3: Matang is close to Bole Port; via Bole Port first-class road and Shuixia Road, the town benefits from port logistics and sea transport. Yueyun buses serve the area near Matang Station with routes to Shuidong, Diancheng, Shalang, and elsewhere, with multiple departures daily for rail and town connections.

economy.title: Economy
economy.p1: Agriculture focuses on rice, peanuts, and sweet potatoes—the town is known as the "granary of Dianbai" and is an important grain producer. Industry includes lock-making, granite quarrying, transport, construction, and catering.
economy.p2: In recent years local fiscal revenue and retail sales have grown steadily. Market-day trade is active, with hundreds of registered businesses. Matang High-Speed Rail Industrial Park leverages transport advantages to attract manufacturing, and rural revitalisation and industrial upgrading are underway.

specialties.title: Local Specialties
specialties.p1: Matang's agricultural specialties include Simiao rice, sweet potatoes, lychees, longan, bayberry wine, cherry tomatoes, and selenium-rich produce. Selenium-rich products have become a key branding focus in recent years, with "selenium-rich Matang" emerging as an important local agricultural label.
specialties.p2: Matang Simiao rice, bayberry wine, Maoming lychees, and Dianbai agarwood have been integrated into rural revitalisation and local branding efforts, becoming important calling cards for the town.

population.title: Population
population.p1: The town has a registered population of about 94,000 and a resident population of about 85,000. The population is predominantly Han Chinese, with a strong sense of community and clan-based settlements.
population.p2: On market days (lunar calendar 3rd, 6th, 9th), the number of visitors can reach 30,000 per day, giving the town a lively commercial and daily life atmosphere.

culture.title: Local Culture
culture.p1: The name Matang ("horse hoof") is said to come from a legend of an immortal's horse leaving deep hoof prints on a large bluestone in the street. The old town has a long history and rich cultural heritage.
culture.p2: Sights such as Tizi Ridge (Liushi Ridge), Jinniu Ridge, Xianshui Qingyu (one of the old Eight Scenes of Dianbai), and Matang People's Park are popular local landmarks and places for leisure.

food.title: Food & People
food.p1: Dianbai's famous dishes can be found in Matang too: Shuidong duck congee, Shuidong white-cut chicken, Shuidong mustard greens, Diancheng fish fritters (oyster fritters), pork offal soup, red-hearted duck eggs, and Baitangying lychees.
food.p2: With mountains and sea nearby, local cuisine combines mountain produce and seafood into a distinct food culture.

customs.title: Customs
customs.p1: Local customs follow western Guangdong and Dianbai traditions: festivals, ancestor worship, dragon boat races, wedding rituals, and market-day gatherings.
customs.p2: Around Changshan Village, temple culture such as Chaowu Temple is preserved alongside the revolutionary sites on Gongluoling, forming a "red and green" blend of local custom and festival atmosphere.

faith.title: Nianli & Faith
faith.p1: Like much of western Guangdong and Dianbai, Matang holds "Nianli" as the most important folk festival of the year—often summed up as "Nianli is bigger than New Year." Nianli combines worship, thanksgiving to the gods, sacrifice, prayer, and feasting. It usually falls between the first and second lunar month; each village has its own date, in three phases: "starting Nianli," "main Nianli," and "Nianli tail," lasting one to three days.
faith.p2: Worship is at the heart of Nianli. Families prepare offerings—meat, wine, dishes, incense—and set them out in the village ("setting the altar"). When the deity statues pass by, a ritual master ("dugong lao") leads the crowd in three kneelings and nine prostrations toward the gods, praying for good weather and peace. Firecrackers line the route to welcome the deities; the mood is both solemn and festive.
faith.p3: The deity parade is the liveliest part of Nianli. Villagers chosen by lot form a procession carrying flags, drums, and gongs, and carry the deity statues through the village. Those who carry the statues are thought to be blessed for the coming year. The parade often includes Taoist gods (wind, rain, thunder, lightning, water, fire) and local figures such as Guan Yu, Kang Wang, Huaguang, and Lady Xian, reflecting the region's "many gods, harmony in diversity" tradition.
faith.p4: Local belief centres on Lingnan sacrificial culture and Lady Xian, alongside Taoism, popular deities, and ancestor worship. Temples such as Chaowu Temple are tied to Nianli, the deity parade, and worship—they are both sacred spaces and the focus of village identity and social life.
faith.p5: Nianli is listed as intangible cultural heritage in Guangdong. Many from Matang return from afar for Nianli—to worship, watch the parade, and feast with family and friends—making it a key bond for local identity and kinship.

liuxiansantai.title: Liu Xian San Tai Temple
liuxiansantai.p1: Liu Xian San Tai Temple stands on Chiling (Xutou Ridge) in Matang Market, and is one of the most representative folk religious sites in Matang Town. It is also the cultural origin of the "sending炮" and "receiving炮" customs of the Matang Nianli festival. According to the Revised Dianbai County Gazetteer, a Liu Santai Temple on Matang Market Ridge was built in the fifth year of the Daoguang reign of the Qing dynasty.
liuxiansantai.p2: Local legend holds that Liu Xian San Tai wandered to Matang in her later years, found the land beautiful and the people kind, and settled on Chiling to cultivate herself and teach the villagers folk songs. After attaining immortality, she continued to protect the community. Locals thus hold a festival on the fifteenth day of the eighth lunar month—her birthday—which gradually evolved into Matang's distinctive Mid-Autumn Nianli. Her cult draws on the Lingnan "Liu Sanjie / Liu Sanmei" singing-immortal tradition, fused with local memories of Chiling, the Horse-Hoof Stone, and community protection.
liuxiansantai.p3: During the festival, Matang holds cannon-sending, cannon-receiving, float parades, lion dances, drum processions, and Yingge dance performances, drawing large crowds of locals and visitors. The central wish is for good harvests, household peace, and community harmony. Liu Xian San Tai Temple is not merely a place of worship—it is the cultural heart of Matang's local memory, village identity, and living folk tradition.

village.title: Changshan Village
village.p1: Changshan Village lies in the hilly belt of Matang Town and is one of the town's most representative revolutionary heritage villages. It preserves Yang Ruifen's former residence, Chaowu Temple, Chaowu Cave, an ancient well, Chaowu Study Hall, Chaowu Academy, Cai Haiguang Ancestral Hall, and the Chiling Revolutionary Martyrs' Monument—combining traditional village character with a rich revolutionary legacy.
village.p2: The village includes at least three natural hamlets: Xingyang Village, Hongshangtou Village, and Jiuxiangtang Village. Settlements are arranged around ridges, farmland, and ponds, creating the classic western Guangdong pattern of "fields in front, ridges behind, village nestled between mountain and field." In recent years, Changshan has been developing red education, eco-tourism, and rural cultural tourism centred on Yang Ruifen's former residence and the Chaowu Temple area.

yangruifen.title: Yang Ruifen & Revolutionary History
yangruifen.p1: Yang Ruifen was born in August 1918 in Xingyang Hamlet, Changshan Village. He joined the revolution in 1940 and the Communist Party of China in 1941, becoming one of the most important revolutionary leaders in the Dianbai area during the War of Resistance and the Liberation War. After graduating from Dianbai Normal School, he returned to teach at Changshan Primary School, where he organised villagers and founded the "Green Sprout Society"—ostensibly a crop-protection group, in reality a vehicle for mobilising the community and building armed capacity.
yangruifen.p2: In March 1945, Yang Ruifen returned to Matang to organise armed resistance; a guerrilla unit ambushed retreating Japanese troops passing through Matang and seized weapons. He went on to serve as head of the Dianbai Guerrilla Force, head of the Dianbai underground Party, secretary of the Dianbai County Party Committee, the first county magistrate of the Dianbai County People's Government, and director of the Zhanjiang Special District Water Conservancy Bureau. He passed away in September 2003.
yangruifen.p3: Yang Ruifen's former residence is located in Xingyang Hamlet, Changshan Village Committee. It is a brick-and-timber complex built roughly 170 years ago and has become an important red cultural landmark in Matang Town, serving as a key site for revolutionary education and Party history study.

gongluoling.title: Gongluoling & Chaowu Cave
gongluoling.p1: Gongluoling lies near Chaowu Temple in Changshan Village. Chaowu Cave on the ridge is an important revolutionary site—it once sheltered Yang Ruifen when he was being hunted. The Republican-era Dianbai County Gazetteer listed Chaowu Cave as one of the Eight Scenes of Dianbai. Local legend also holds that Lady Xian once used the site to station troops and store supplies, adding an earlier heroic dimension to the ridge.
gongluoling.p2: Gongluoling is more than a natural hill—it is a cultural space that weaves together revolutionary memory, folk legend, and local faith. Chaowu Temple, Chaowu Cave, and Gongluoling together form the most representative mountain cultural landscape of Changshan Village, and are key landmarks for understanding Matang's revolutionary history and local culture.
